message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Bubble CPAP may be used in infants with suspected or confirmed COVID-19. Electrostatic filters may reduce cross-infection. This study aims to determine if including a filter in the bubble CPAP circuit impacts stability of pressure delivery.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    A new electrostatic filter was placed before (pre) or after (post) the bubble CPAP generator, or with no filter (control) in an
                    <jats:italic>in vitro</jats:italic>
                    study. Pressure was recorded at the nasal interface for 18 h (6 L/min; 7 cmH
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    O) on three occasions for each configuration. Filter failure was defined as pressure >9 cmH
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    0 for 60 continuous minutes. The filter was weighed before and after each experiment.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Mean (SD) time to reach the fail-point was 257 (116) min and 525 (566) min for filter placement pre- and post-CPAP generator, respectively. Mean pressure was higher throughout in the pre-generator position compared to control. The filter weight was heavier at study end in the pre-compared to the post-generator position.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Placement of the filter at the pre-generator position in a bubble CPAP circuit should be avoided due to unstable mean pressure. Filters are likely to become saturated with water over time. The post-generator position may accommodate a filter, but regular pressure monitoring and early replacement are required.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Electrostatic filters to reduce COVID-19 spread in bubble CPAP: an in vitro study of safety and efficacy
